does: This paper investigates the internal mechanisms of Walrus, a foundation model for continuum dynamics, using mechanistic interpretability techniques guided by physical principles. The authors apply sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to probe a selected layer of the model and use enstrophy, a physically grounded metric, to triage over 20,000 learned features. By examining how the model represents shear flow across different parameter settings, they identify piecewise consistency in feature recruitment, where specific subsets of features recur across setups in similar functional roles. The study reveals that while the model successfully reproduces known continuum dynamics, the internal mechanisms show only partial alignment with classical physics, indicating that the model achieves accurate predictions through approaches that deviate from explicit physical reasoning.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to understand deep learning models in scientific domains through mechanistic interpretability, extending prior research applying SAEs to large neural networks. It responds to the broader challenge of interpretability in foundation models, particularly in contexts where strong ground truth exists. The paper connects to prior work on neural network interpretability in physics-informed learning, interpretability of transformer models, and the emerging field of mechanistic interpretability applied to scientific emulators. It addresses the practical question raised by prior studies examining whether neural networks learn physically meaningful representations when tasked with scientific prediction, building on methods that use domain-specific metrics to guide feature analysis rather than relying purely on statistical properties.
enables: This work opens several research directions in scientific interpretability and neural network analysis. It establishes a framework for using physically grounded metrics to interpret large feature spaces in scientific models, potentially applicable to other foundation models in physics and engineering domains. The identification of piecewise consistency suggests future work on understanding feature modularity and generalization in scientific neural networks. The partially non-physical internal mechanisms revealed suggest investigations into whether alternative training procedures, architectures, or inductive biases could align model representations more closely with known physics while maintaining predictive accuracy. Furthermore, this case study could motivate broader auditing practices for deployed scientific emulators, helping practitioners understand when and why such models might fail on out-of-distribution problems.
